text:
2026/4/2 10:39 Artificial Intelligence (2026 Spring)
Artificial Intelligence (2026
Spring)
UVA CS_4710-002
Table of Contents
Course Description
How do self-driving cars navigate on the road, or chatbots (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, ...)
determine what to say? At the core of the technological transformation is the increasing
ability of machines to learn, reason, and interact intelligently with complex data, humans,
and the world. Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the core technology behind this
transformation, allowing everything from our mobile phones and smart homes to robots
and medical diagnostic tools to understand complex information and act intelligently.
But AI is about so much more. Have you ever wondered: Can machines truly learn,
understand, and even create? What separates today's AI from mere automation? And
how do we ensure these intelligent technologies serve humanity ethically? This course
will explore and apply the core concepts of AI, from intelligent agents and search to
machine learning and generative AI. You'll gain hands-on experience through class
exercises and homework assignments. You'll solidify your understanding of AI concepts
with weekly review questions and midterms. A significant component of this course is
the project, where you'll have the opportunity to design, implement, and evaluate an AI
system for a problem you found at UVA/Charlottesville.
Prerequisite: CS 2150 or CS 3100 with a grade of C- or better.
Time and Location
https://ylkuo.notion.site/Artificial-Intelligence-2026-Spring-2e1c38a2133680358338ff5127bfde18 1/11

2026/4/2 10:39 Artificial Intelligence (2026 Spring)
• TuTh 11:00 am - 12:15 pm
• Olsson 009
Teaching Team
• Instructor: Yen-Ling Kuo
◦ Email: ylkuo@virginia.edu
◦ Office Hour: Wednesdays, 11-11:45 am or by appointment, Olsson 261
• TA: Xuhui (Joshua) Kang • TA: Rui Shen
◦ Email: qhv6ku@virginia.edu ◦ Email: kqn9mt@virginia.edu
◦ Office Hour: Fridays, 5-6pm, ◦ Office Hour: Tuesdays, 4-5pm,
Rice 232 or Zoom (see Canvas) Rice 232
• TA: Chen Gong
◦ Email: fzv6en@virginia.edu
◦ Office Hour: Thursdays, 4-5pm,
Rice 442
Course Format
The course will be delivered through lectures. In addition to two lectures a week,
students will have weekly review questions, 5 programming homework assignments, two
midterm exams, and a final project to practice the concepts presented in the lectures.
Textbook (optional)
• Russell and Norvig. Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach. 3rd Edition. 2010. (or
4th Edition)
Details and grading policy
https://ylkuo.notion.site/Artificial-Intelligence-2026-Spring-2e1c38a2133680358338ff5127bfde18 2/11

2026/4/2 10:39 Artificial Intelligence (2026 Spring)
• Weekly review questions: 5%
◦ Weekly review questions will help you review the key concepts of lectures and
course material. Review questions will be offered at the end of most weeks in a
multiple-choice format on Gradescope.
◦ You can review them at your own pace after class. Questions will be available for
a week, no extension. Only your top five scores will be used in the final grade.
• Participation: 5%
◦ As AI is evolving very quickly, we encourage you to have continuous
participation within and outside of lectures in any format to keep engaging in
or learning about AI. This includes, but is not limited to, the following activities.
▪ In-class activities (responses submitted through online forms), AI news/tips
sharing (up to two students each lecture), Piazza discussions (including
questions and answering about AI, not logistics), scribe lectures (up to two
scribers per lecture), and participate in AI activities (hackathons,
talks/seminars, etc.) outside of class.
◦ You can participate in any format that works best for you. At least ten activities
are needed to have full participation credit in the final grade.
• Homework: 30%
◦ Homework assignments are designed to provide you with individual hands-on
practice to translate theoretical knowledge into working code (in Python). You
will be implementing various types of AI agents to play a game like
Minesweeper! In each assignment, we provide you with a description of agent
design and skeleton code. You will apply the foundational concepts and critical
thinking you learned to complete the AI agents.
◦ Late submission is allowed for three days after the deadline with a 20%
deduction per day. We will automatically grant two slip days for each student
throughout the semester.
▪ Slip day: Using slip days avoids the late penalty. You can use your two slip
days in one homework (two days) or two homeworks (one day each
homework).
https://ylkuo.notion.site/Artificial-Intelligence-2026-Spring-2e1c38a2133680358338ff5127bfde18 3/11

2026/4/2 10:39 Artificial Intelligence (2026 Spring)
• Midterm: 30%
◦ The midterm exams will serve as a comprehensive assessment to check your
understanding of core AI concepts and methods. It will be an open-note exam,
including multiple-choice questions and short-answer questions. Questions will
focus on the concept, algorithm, and simple calculations.
• Course project: 30%
◦ The course project challenges you to integrate various AI concepts into a
cohesive solution for a real-world problem, a problem you found in
UVA/Charlottesville! Working in teams, you will navigate the full problem-solving
lifecycle, from identifying appropriate AI methods and leveraging libraries to
researching relevant resources. This hands-on experience is critical for
developing the confidence and practical skills needed to tackle complex AI
challenges beyond the classroom.
Final project guidelines
• Topic: Solving problems in UVA or Charlottesville with AI
◦ Identify a problem you or students face in UVA/Charlottesville, design an AI
agent, and apply the AI or generative AI methods taught in the class to
implement the designed AI agent to solve the problem.
• Details:
Final Project Guideline

• Gradescope: https://www.gradescope.com/courses/1220922
◦ Weekly review questions, Homework submissions

on Apr 8 for

Thu, Apr 16 Image generation

Tue, Apr 21

(Sat)
Week 16 Course end

Week 16 Project: Final
Thu, Apr 30 report due
Generative AI Policy
In this course, we will explore different topics in AI, and of course, how to use generative
AI tools is of interest. We also have some opportunities for you to practice with these
tools. I encourage you to explore generative AI tools to help your learning, but very
importantly, use them with a critical and responsible view. That means
• DO NOT simply copy the review questions, homework assignments, and lecture
videos to ask AI to generate answers and code to submit without working on them
yourself.
• DO NOT use AI to directly generate reports without thinking about the project and
working on it yourself.
• DO discuss with AI to clarify or explain the concepts you do not understand well,
and if uncertain, ask and discuss on Piazza or during office hours!
https://ylkuo.notion.site/Artificial-Intelligence-2026-Spring-2e1c38a2133680358338ff5127bfde18 7/11

2026/4/2 10:39 Artificial Intelligence (2026 Spring)
• DO brainstorm ideas with AI, but critically think about whether the ideas make sense
or how you may use or implement them.
• DO use AI to improve your writing, such as proofreading, grammar/spellchecking,
suggesting organization of content, etc, but review them to learn what is edited and
if you agree with them.
The goal of using AI is to help you learn and provide you with more ways to learn at your
own pace, but the goal is not to outsource your brain to AI and not to do your
coursework (this is exactly replacing yourself with an AI!)
If you use any generative AI tools in your homework assignments and projects in any
way, you are required to acknowledge and cite all generative AI use/contributions.
• Create an appendix at the end of your submission with the following information:
◦ The name of the platforms you used
◦ The exact prompts or conversations you had with AI
◦ The raw AI output (if it answers)
◦ Any iterations or adaptations you have to improve the outputs
◦ Explain how you use the output or what you find the AI fell short on
Statements
Honor
I trust every student in this course to fully comply with all of the provisions of the
University’s Honor Code. By enrolling in this course, you have agreed to abide by and
uphold the Honor System of the University of Virginia, as well as the following policies
specific to this course.
• All graded assignments must be pledged.
• All suspected violations will be forwarded to the Honor Committee, and you may, at
my discretion, receive an immediate zero on that assignment regardless of any
action taken by the Honor Committee.
https://ylkuo.notion.site/Artificial-Intelligence-2026-Spring-2e1c38a2133680358338ff5127bfde18 8/11

2026/4/2 10:39 Artificial Intelligence (2026 Spring)
Please let me know if you have any questions regarding the course Honor policy. If you
believe you may have committed an Honor Offense, you may wish to file a
Conscientious Retraction by calling the Honor Offices at (434) 924-7602. For your
retraction to be considered valid, it must, among other things, be filed with the Honor
Committee before you are aware that the act in question has come under suspicion by
anyone. More information can be found at http://honor.virginia.edu. Your Honor
representatives can be found at: http://honor.virginia.edu/representatives.
Students with disabilities or learning needs
It is my goal to create a learning experience that is as accessible as possible. If you
anticipate any issues related to the format, materials, or requirements of this course,
please meet with me outside of class so we can explore potential options. Students with
disabilities may also wish to work with the Student Disability Access Center (SDAC) to
discuss a range of options to removing barriers in this course, including official
accommodations. We are fortunate to have an SDAC advisor, Courtney MacMasters,
physically located in Engineering. You may email her at cmacmasters@virginia.edu to
schedule an appointment. For general questions please visit the SDAC
website: sdac.studenthealth.virginia.edu. If you have already been approved for
accommodations through SDAC, please send me your accommodation letter and meet
with me so we can develop an implementation plan together.
Religious accommodations
It is the University's long-standing policy and practice to reasonably accommodate
students so that they do not experience an adverse academic consequence when
sincerely held religious beliefs or observances conflict with academic requirements.
Students who wish to request academic accommodation for a religious observance
should submit their request to me by email as far in advance as possible. Students who
have questions or concerns about academic accommodations for religious observance
or religious beliefs may contact the University’s Office for Equal Opportunity and Civil
Rights (EOCR) at UVAEOCR@virginia.edu or 434-924-3200.
Harassment, Discrimination, and Interpersonal Violence
https://ylkuo.notion.site/Artificial-Intelligence-2026-Spring-2e1c38a2133680358338ff5127bfde18 9/11

2026/4/2 10:39 Artificial Intelligence (2026 Spring)
The University of Virginia is dedicated to providing a safe and equitable learning
environment for all students. If you or someone you know has been affected by power-
based personal violence, more information can be found on the UVA Sexual Violence
website that describes reporting options and resources available -
www.virginia.edu/sexualviolence.
The same resources and options for individuals who experience sexual misconduct are
available for discrimination, harassment, and retaliation. UVA prohibits discrimination
and harassment based on age, color, disability, family medical or genetic information,
gender identity or expression, marital status, military status, national or ethnic origin,
political affiliation, pregnancy (including childbirth and related conditions), race, religion,
sex, sexual orientation, or veteran status. UVA policy also prohibits retaliation for
reporting such behavior.
If you witness or are aware of someone who has experienced prohibited conduct, you
are encouraged to submit a report to Just Report It (justreportit.virginia.edu) or contact
EOCR, the office of Equal Opportunity and Civil Rights.
If you would prefer to disclose such conduct to a confidential resource where what you
share is not reported to the University, you can turn to Counseling & Psychological
Services (“CAPS”) and Women’s Center Counseling Staff and Confidential Advocates (for
students of all genders).
As your professor and as a person, know that I care about you and your well-being and
stand ready to provide support and resources as I can. As a faculty member, I am a
responsible employee, which means that I am required by University policy and by
federal law to report certain kinds of conduct that you report to me to the University's
Title IX Coordinator. The Title IX Coordinator's job is to ensure that the reporting
student receives the resources and support that they need, while also determining
whether further action is necessary to ensure survivor safety and the safety of the
University community.
Support for your career development
https://ylkuo.notion.site/Artificial-Intelligence-2026-Spring-2e1c38a2133680358338ff5127bfde18 10/11

2026/4/2 10:39 Artificial Intelligence (2026 Spring)
Engaging in your career development is an important part of your student experience.
For example, presenting at a research conference, attending an interview for a job or
internship, or participating in an extern/shadowing experience are not only necessary
steps on your path but are also invaluable lessons in and of themselves. I wish to
encourage and support you in activities related to your career development. To that end,
please notify me by email as far in advance as possible to arrange for appropriate
accommodations.
Student support team
You have many resources available to you when you experience academic or personal
stresses. In addition to your professor, the School of Engineering and Applied Science
has staff members located in Thornton Hall who you can contact to help manage
academic or personal challenges. Please do not wait until the end of the semester to ask
for help!
Learning
Lisa Lampe, Assistant Dean for Undergraduate Affairs
Georgina Nembhard, Director of Student Success
Courtney MacMasters, Accessibility Specialist
Free tutoring is available for most classes.
Health and Wellbeing
Kelly Garrett, Assistant Dean of Students, Student Safety and Support
https://ylkuo.notion.site/Artificial-Intelligence-2026-Spring-2e1c38a2133680358338ff5127bfde18 11/11

Honor
I trust every student in this course to fully comply with all of the provisions of the
University’s Honor Code. By enrolling in this course, you have agreed to abide by and
uphold the Honor System of the University of Virginia, as well as the following policies
specific to this course.
• All graded assignments must be pledged.
• All suspected violations will be forwarded to the Honor Committee, and you may, at
my discretion, receive an immediate zero on that assignment regardless of any
action taken by the Honor Committee.

Please let me know if you have any questions regarding the course Honor policy. If you
believe you may have committed an Honor Offense, you may wish to file a
Conscientious Retraction by calling the Honor Offices at (434) 924-7602. For your
retraction to be considered valid, it must, among other things, be filed with the Honor
Committee before you are aware that the act in question has come under suspicion by
anyone. More information can be found at http://honor.virginia.edu. Your Honor
representatives can be found at: http://honor.virginia.edu/representatives.
Students with disabilities or learning needs
It is my goal to create a learning experience that is as accessible as possible. If you
anticipate any issues related to the format, materials, or requirements of this course,
please meet with me outside of class so we can explore potential options. Students with
disabilities may also wish to work with the Student Disability Access Center (SDAC) to
discuss a range of options to removing barriers in this course, including official
accommodations. We are fortunate to have an SDAC advisor, Courtney MacMasters,
physically located in Engineering. You may email her at cmacmasters@virginia.edu to
schedule an appointment. For general questions please visit the SDAC
website: sdac.studenthealth.virginia.edu. If you have already been approved for
accommodations through SDAC, please send me your accommodation letter and meet
with me so we can develop an implementation plan together.
Religious accommodations
It is the University's long-standing policy and practice to reasonably accommodate
students so that they do not experience an adverse academic consequence when
sincerely held religious beliefs or observances conflict with academic requirements.
Students who wish to request academic accommodation for a religious observance
should submit their request to me by email as far in advance as possible. Students who
have questions or concerns about academic accommodations for religious observance
or religious beliefs may contact the University’s Office for Equal Opportunity and Civil
Rights (EOCR) at UVAEOCR@virginia.edu or 434-924-3200.
Harassment, Discrimination, and Interpersonal Violence

2026/4/2 10:39 Artificial Intelligence (2026 Spring)
The University of Virginia is dedicated to providing a safe and equitable learning
environment for all students. If you or someone you know has been affected by power-
based personal violence, more information can be found on the UVA Sexual Violence
website that describes reporting options and resources available -
www.virginia.edu/sexualviolence.
The same resources and options for individuals who experience sexual misconduct are
available for discrimination, harassment, and retaliation. UVA prohibits discrimination
and harassment based on age, color, disability, family medical or genetic information,
gender identity or expression, marital status, military status, national or ethnic origin,
political affiliation, pregnancy (including childbirth and related conditions), race, religion,
sex, sexual orientation, or veteran status. UVA policy also prohibits retaliation for
reporting such behavior.
If you witness or are aware of someone who has experienced prohibited conduct, you
are encouraged to submit a report to Just Report It (justreportit.virginia.edu) or contact
EOCR, the office of Equal Opportunity and Civil Rights.
If you would prefer to disclose such conduct to a confidential resource where what you
share is not reported to the University, you can turn to Counseling & Psychological
Services (“CAPS”) and Women’s Center Counseling Staff and Confidential Advocates (for
students of all genders).
As your professor and as a person, know that I care about you and your well-being and
stand ready to provide support and resources as I can. As a faculty member, I am a
responsible employee, which means that I am required by University policy and by
federal law to report certain kinds of conduct that you report to me to the University's
Title IX Coordinator. The Title IX Coordinator's job is to ensure that the reporting
student receives the resources and support that they need, while also determining
whether further action is necessary to ensure survivor safety and the safety of the
University community.

Engaging in your career development is an important part of your student experience.
For example, presenting at a research conference, attending an interview for a job or
internship, or participating in an extern/shadowing experience are not only necessary
steps on your path but are also invaluable lessons in and of themselves. I wish to
encourage and support you in activities related to your career development. To that end,
please notify me by email as far in advance as possible to arrange for appropriate
accommodations.
Student support team
You have many resources available to you when you experience academic or personal
stresses. In addition to your professor, the School of Engineering and Applied Science
has staff members located in Thornton Hall who you can contact to help manage
academic or personal challenges. Please do not wait until the end of the semester to ask
for help!
